questions.q: A vertex operator $Y(a, z)$ associated to a state $a$ in a VOA is:
choices: a single linear operator on the state space $V$
a formal Laurent series $\sum_n a_n z^{-n-1}$ whose coefficients are linear operators on $V$
a holomorphic function on the punctured disc valued in $V$
an element of the Lie algebra of automorphisms of $V$
questions.explain: $Y(\cdot, z) : V \to \mathrm{End}(V)[[z, z^{-1}]]$ is a state–field correspondence: each $a \in V$ gives the formal series $Y(a, z) = \sum_{n \in \mathbb{Z}} a_n z^{-n-1}$. The mode $a_n$ is the coefficient of $z^{-n-1}$, so $a_{-1}\mathbf{1} = a$ recovers the state.

questions.q: The locality axiom for two vertex operators $Y(a, z)$ and $Y(b, w)$ says:
choices: $Y(a, z) Y(b, w) = Y(b, w) Y(a, z)$ as formal series
there exists $N \gg 0$ with $(z - w)^N [Y(a, z), Y(b, w)] = 0$
$Y(a, z) Y(b, w)$ converges absolutely for $|z| > |w|$
questions.explain: Two fields are mutually local if some power $(z-w)^N$ kills their commutator. This is weaker than commutativity: it allows OPE singularities at $z = w$ but forces them to be of finite order, recovering an algebraic shadow of the physicists' radial-ordering.

questions.q: If $Y(a, z) = \sum_n a_n z^{-n-1}$, what is the index $n$ of the mode whose action on the vacuum returns $a$ — i.e. the $n$ for which $a_n \mathbf{1} = a$?
questions.explain: By the creation axiom, $Y(a, z)\mathbf{1}|_{z=0} = a$. Reading off the constant term in $z$ (the coefficient of $z^0$) and matching against $\sum_n a_n z^{-n-1}$ forces $-n - 1 = 0$, so $n = -1$. Thus $a = a_{-1}\mathbf{1}$, the standard 'state-from-mode' identification.

voa-axioms.title: VOA axioms — vacuum, translation, locality, Jacobi

questions.q: The vacuum axiom of a VOA requires that:
choices: $Y(\mathbf{1}, z) = \mathrm{id}_V$, the constant series of the identity operator
questions.explain: The vacuum vector $\mathbf{1}$ is the unit: $Y(\mathbf{1}, z) = \mathrm{id}_V$ (no $z$-dependence). The companion axiom $Y(a, z)\mathbf{1} \in V[[z]]$ with $Y(a, z)\mathbf{1}|_{z=0} = a$ encodes the state–field correspondence.

questions.q: The translation operator $T$ in a VOA satisfies:
questions.explain: Translation covariance: differentiating with respect to the formal variable $z$ is realised by the inner derivation $[T, -]$. The infinitesimal translation $T$ is identified with $L_{-1}$ once a Virasoro element is fixed.

questions.q: Why is the Borcherds–Jacobi identity often preferred over locality plus the vacuum axiom as the definition of a VOA?
choices: It is strictly weaker, allowing more examples
It packages locality, associativity, and skew-symmetry into a single equation among modes, which is convenient for proofs
It avoids any reference to formal power series
It is the only axiom that makes sense in positive characteristic
questions.explain: The Jacobi identity $\sum (\cdots) Y(a, z_1) Y(b, z_2) c = \cdots$ is equivalent to locality plus weak associativity (under standard hypotheses) and reduces to a single mode-level relation. It makes algebraic manipulations — computing OPE coefficients, deriving commutator formulas — purely combinatorial.

questions.q: The Virasoro algebra commutation relation in a VOA reads:
questions.explain: The Virasoro algebra is the unique non-trivial central extension of the Witt algebra of vector fields on $S^1$. The cocycle $\tfrac{c}{12}(m^3 - m)\,\delta_{m+n,0}$ scales with the central charge $c$, vanishing on $L_{-1}, L_0, L_1$ (the $\mathfrak{sl}_2$ subalgebra) since $m^3 - m = m(m-1)(m+1)$.

questions.q: For the central charge of the moonshine module $V^\natural$, what is $c$?
questions.explain: $V^\natural$ has central charge $c = 24$. This is forced by modular invariance: the graded character $\tr q^{L_0 - c/24}$ must transform like a weight-$0$ modular function on $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$, which requires the shift $-c/24 = -1$ so that $L_0 - c/24$ has integer eigenvalues starting at $-1$, matching $J(\tau) = q^{-1} + 196884\, q + \cdots$.

questions.q: The conformal vector $\omega \in V$ supplies the operators $L_n$ via:
questions.explain: By convention $Y(\omega, z) = \sum_n L_n z^{-n - 2}$ — the shift by $2$ comes from $\omega$ having conformal weight $2$ (it is the stress–energy tensor in CFT language). Then $L_0$ is the coefficient of $z^{-2}$ and acts as the grading operator.

questions.q: Zhu's theorem (1996) states that for a $C_2$-cofinite rational VOA $V$, the graded characters $\mathrm{ch}_M(\tau) = \tr_M q^{L_0 - c/24}$ of irreducible modules:
choices: form the components of a vector-valued modular form for $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$, with the modules permuted by the modular action
questions.explain: Zhu showed that the span of the graded characters of irreducible $V$-modules is preserved by the $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$-action $\tau \mapsto (a\tau + b)/(c\tau + d)$; together they assemble into a vector-valued modular form. Individual characters mix under $S : \tau \mapsto -1/\tau$ — that mixing is the fusion-rules data.

questions.q: The shift by $-c/24$ in $\tr q^{L_0 - c/24}$ is necessary because:
choices: it adjusts the modular weight so the character has good $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$ transformation properties (the $\eta$-function shift)
it makes the leading coefficient equal $1$
it is a normalisation choice with no mathematical content
questions.explain: The $-c/24$ shift comes from the Casimir of the Virasoro vacuum representation; it is exactly the shift in $\eta(\tau) = q^{1/24} \prod(1 - q^n)$. Without it, the character is a $q$-series with no good modular transformation; with it, the character lives on the modular curve $X(1)$ rather than its universal cover.

choices: the quotient $V / C_2(V)$ is finite-dimensional, where $C_2(V)$ is spanned by $a_{-2} b$ for $a, b \in V$
$V$ has only countably many irreducible modules
$V$ admits a non-degenerate invariant bilinear form
questions.explain: $C_2(V)$ is the span of $\{a_{-2} b : a, b \in V\}$; finite-dimensionality of $V / C_2(V)$ is a strong finiteness condition that, combined with rationality, gives Zhu's theorem and the Verlinde formula. Every VOA appearing in moonshine satisfies it.

questions.q: The moonshine module $V^\natural$ of Frenkel–Lepowsky–Meurman is:
choices: a finite-dimensional representation of the Monster
a $\mathbb{Z}$-graded VOA of central charge $24$ whose graded dimension is $J(\tau) = j(\tau) - 744$
the Lie algebra of the Monster
the affinisation of the Leech lattice
questions.explain: $V^\natural$ is built as a $\mathbb{Z}/2$-orbifold of the lattice VOA on the Leech lattice. Its graded dimension is $\sum_n (\dim V^\natural_n)\, q^n = J(\tau)$, and the Monster $\mathbb{M}$ acts as its full automorphism group — the cleanest realisation of McKay's observation.

questions.q: The Conway–Norton conjecture, proved by Borcherds, asserts that for each $g \in \mathbb{M}$ the McKay–Thompson series $T_g(\tau) = \sum_n \tr(g \mid V^\natural_n)\, q^n$ is:
choices: the Hauptmodul of a genus-zero subgroup of $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{R})$ commensurable with $\mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{Z})$
questions.explain: For each conjugacy class $[g] \subset \mathbb{M}$, $T_g$ is the Hauptmodul of a specific genus-zero subgroup $\Gamma_g \subset \mathrm{SL}_2(\mathbb{R})$. The 'genus-zero' constraint is what made the conjecture so striking: it pins out a finite list of subgroups out of an infinite family.

questions.q: Borcherds' proof routes through the Monster Lie algebra, which is built from $V^\natural$ via:
choices: the BRST cohomology of the Goddard–Thorn no-ghost theorem
questions.explain: Borcherds tensors $V^\natural$ with a rank-$2$ Lorentzian-lattice VOA, then applies the Goddard–Thorn no-ghost theorem to extract a generalised Kac–Moody (Borcherds–Kac–Moody) Lie algebra. Its denominator identity furnishes the replication formulas that pin each Thompson series to its Conway–Norton Hauptmodul.

voa-applications.title: Applications — chiral CFT and chiral algebras

questions.q: In two-dimensional conformal field theory, a vertex operator algebra encodes:
choices: the chiral (holomorphic) half of the theory; the antichiral half is a separate VOA
the spectrum of conformal weights up to integer shifts
questions.explain: A 2D CFT factorises (subject to modular invariance) into a chiral and an antichiral half. Each half is a VOA — the same axioms that codify locality of fields on $S^1$ at fixed time. The full theory is a tensor product of the two halves modulo gluing data.

questions.q: Beilinson–Drinfeld's chiral algebras generalise VOAs by:
choices: replacing formal power series with $\mathcal{D}$-modules on an algebraic curve
questions.explain: A chiral algebra on a curve $X$ is a $\mathcal{D}$-module on $X$ with a chiral product on $\mathcal{A} \boxtimes \mathcal{A}$ over $X \times X$ off the diagonal. Restriction to the formal disc near a point recovers a VOA; the global data is what makes geometric Langlands work.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT an example of a vertex operator algebra?
choices: the lattice VOA $V_L$ for an even positive-definite lattice $L$
the affine Kac–Moody VOA at a positive integer level
the universal enveloping algebra of an arbitrary finite-dimensional Lie algebra
questions.explain: Lattice, affine Kac–Moody (at admissible levels), and Heisenberg VOAs are the standard VOA constructions. The universal enveloping algebra $U(\mathfrak{g})$ of an arbitrary finite-dimensional Lie algebra is an associative algebra, not a VOA — to get a VOA from $\mathfrak{g}$ you affinise to $\widehat{\mathfrak{g}}$ and pass to a vacuum module at a chosen level.
